rulings.Case Title: MELVIN RODRICK ET AL VS. SOCO WEST, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR WITHDRAWAL OF ATTORNEY OF RECORD
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, February 6, 2024, in Department 301, Line 1. Gordon Rees Scully Mansukhani LLP's Motion to Be Relieved as Counsel is GRANTED. No opposition filed. Gordon Rees Scully Mansukhani is to follow C.R.C. rule 3.1362(e) and prepare the order on Form MC-053. If a hearing is requested, it will be on February 6, 2024 at 9:30 a.m. in Department 301, the Hon. Rochelle C. East presiding. Attorneys may appear in person or remotely. Face coverings are optional. Remote appearances must be arranged through CourtCall (1-888-88-COURT). No prior notice or permission is required. If a party wishes to have the matter reported, the parties must meet and confer to agree on only one court reporter, who must be licensed under Bus. & Prof. Code, sec. 8016. There will be only one official record. If the parties cannot agree, the Court will designate a qualified court reporter to provide the official transcript for the matter, and the party or parties will bear the cost. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must email contestasbestostr@sftc.org by 4:30 p.m. stating their intention to contest. The prevailing party must prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. If contested, the proposed order must be submitted to the Court after the hearing. If not contested, the proposed order may be submitted before or after the hearing. = (301/RCE)

rulings.Case Title: GLEN SCHUETZ ET AL VS. RILEY POWER INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, February 6, 2024, in Department 301, Line 2. Defendant Scott Co. of California's Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiffs. Reply filed by Defendant. 1. Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden of showing Plaintiff does not possess and cannot reasonably obtain needed evidence of asbestos exposure and causation. (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855.) The Court need not rule on Defendant's immaterial objections to Plaintiff's opposing evidence under C.C.P. section 437c(q). 2. With respect to Glen Schuetz's deposition, the Court infers he has no personal knowledge of asbestos exposure. (Andrews v. Foster Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 96, 104; Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439.) But this does not create an inference that Plaintiffs' evidence is factually devoid in its entirety. (Andrews, supra, 138 Cal.App.4th at p. 104; Weber, supra, 143 Cal.App.4th at p. 1439.) The Court must review any special discovery responses included in the moving evidence. (Andrews, supra, 138 Cal.App.4th at pp. 104-107.) 3. With respect to special discovery, Defendant argues that "as a matter of law . . . plaintiffs' responses to comprehensive discovery must fully disclose the evidence known to them at the time of their responses." (Andrews v. Foster Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 96, 106.) The Court agrees. Plaintiffs suggest the length of their responses is sufficient to presume factual specificity, and that no more is required of them. (See, e.g., Plaintiffs' Response to Defendant's Undisputed Material Fact No. 7.) It is well established that "[p]arties have a duty to respond to discovery requests as completely and straightforwardly as possible given the information available to them." (Andrews, supra, 138 Cal.App.4th at p. 106 [citation omitted].) "When defendants conduct comprehensive discovery, plaintiffs cannot play 'hide the ball' . . . ." (Ibid.) 4. Plaintiffs' responses tend to lack specific facts with respect to their identified witnesses and documents and a nexus between Glen Schuetz's asbestos exposure and Defendant's role in causing the exposure. For example, Plaintiffs list Defendant-specific documents without providing context as to the material facts contained in such documents. (See Nazari Dec., Ex. C [Plaintiffs' Responses to Defendant's Special Interrogatories, Set One, Dated Nov. 29, 2022] at 7:6-10:28 and Ex. E [Plaintiffs' Responses to Defendant's Request for Production of Documents, Set One, dated Nov. 29, 2022] at 5:19-9:22; see also Andrews, supra, 138 Cal.App.4th at p. 106.) However, Defendant encloses only limited excerpts of Plaintiffs' special discovery responses. (See Nazari Dec., Ex. C and Ex. E.) The Court cannot find Defendant has met its initial burden under the current evidentiary showing. (Tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 2.)
